item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Previous edit reverted, as I feel it clashed tonally with the rest of the article and its intent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
au where Madabout Video Games's suicide is an actual suicide.

oh wait. I can do that myself.

item.title: Perfect
item.content:encoded: This is the article that inspired me to get an account on the site so I could give it a +1. The writing is perfect, the character development is perfect, the story is perfect. Heather and her tales are probably my favorite pieces of fiction I've read in the past few years. Perfectly captures identity struggles and even though I don't tick all the boxes Heather does, she's still a super relatable character and I'm metaphorically foaming at the mouth waiting for more content lskdjg. Thank you for this, seriously.
Don't know when this hit 200, but sincerely thank you to everyone who enjoyed my favorite article that I've worked on. Means the world to me : )
There are so many reasons to give this a +1, but I'm especially biased to the fact that you guys used "Super High School Level" instead of "Ultimate." Fuck yeah. Great skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not a huge Gamers Against weed fan but articles like this are really changing my opinion of them as a GOI. I see why this is controversial but I really liked this; for me it invoked relatable "video-games as escapism" and feelings of being lost/confused that really vibed with me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i liked this a lot!! i was thinking at the start of the article that heather and chiaki would be great friends, and then you actually put in danganronpa references!!! definite +1 for great writing :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No inside jokes or what-have-you in this article. The title is a reference to a Homestuck song, because I like Homestuck and wanted to reference a gamer girl with another gamer girl's theme.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just didn’t do anything for me. Nothing really hooked me about it, I feel like I’m missing the joke or the reference or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This didn't do anything for me, mostly for reasons that Shaggydredlocks has elucidated very eloquently up-thread. Shaggy's thematic criticisms aside, the anomaly itself is really finicky and kind of difficult to understand at a glance, without really rewarding the reader or having enough narrative weight to justify it. As a result, the test logs often ended up feeling superfluous and being a bit of a drag to read.

item.title: Re: Too Human?
item.content:encoded:
Yeah… raise your hands if you've seen the TV-head trope before.

An objecthead isn't a story trope, although if we wanna get really into it, anything and everything ever can be a trope. A trope, I feel, doesn't automatically disqualify an article from being excellent or creative, but a tool authors can use to their advantages.

I'll give you your critique on this feeling too "human", but I want to deliberately avoid the 2008 - 2013 (arguably stretching into 2014 as well) period of time where humanoid anomalies were largely not viewed as human even by their readers. Vast amounts of authors have written countless amounts of articles since then (before this article even) where the reader is meant to empathize with a humanoid anomaly.

Thank you for your critique, but unfortunately, I will not be changing a single aspect in regards to either point you bring up.

item.title: Too Human?
item.content:encoded:
Yeah… raise your hands if you've seen the TV-head trope before. This article also treats the object too much like a human.

As far as I'm concerned, even humanoid SCP objects need to avoid feeling too "human"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: you can do anythign without my permission

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
thanks i chose the crosslinks

(i'm a little out of it right now but i'll try to give a proper response later)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, this was fantastic. 1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm kinda split on this.

On one hand, I see why some blokes dislikes this. Shaggy made a very good comment that got into this better than I could explaining some of this skips faults.

On the other, this is executed pretty finely and its hard for me to hate on that

IMO, this does what it does well, but I do not like what it does which is driving me to no vote this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mr. Thinks Monty Python Is the Only Source of Humor in the Current Year

Yeah well you're a stupid poopypants. >.>

We're knights of the round table/We dance whene'er we're able

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some people? Ruin the SCP wiki?? To cope???

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
gay/10

soros shall be pleased

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it wrong? 🤔🤔🤔

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3090 is the absolute proof that the SCP Wiki has gone to total shit.

This is the best SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Had to make some minor edits since I wasn't wholly satisfied with how this looked.
best article of 2017 :kappa:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: PepperPixel made this super cute short animatic for 3090 and I'm gonna cry about how cute it is, YouTube here (warning for bright flashing colors). I hereby declare Kero Kero Bonito to be her official soundtrack (that is, if Ben is also fine with declaring such).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a pretty good example of how a humanoid should work. I can see myself using this in future critiques as a role model.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In my head she sounds like Karen from Spongebob.

item.title: Re: Downvoted.
item.content:encoded:
After rereading and some deliberation, I've changed my vote to neutral.

There are still a lot of things about this SCP I don't like.
- The characterization at times seems a bit inconsistent and confused (I still don't understand how she hates violent games but loves danganronpa)
- she doesn't really fit with my personal view of GAW (which is that they do their best to either be harmless or help people; this just seems like a bad deal for everyone involved, Heather included).
All that being said, the article is well written. It conveys most of the information in a clear manner, and keeps the clinical tone of the foundation very well.

TLDR: I still don't like it, but in retrospect I might have overreacted, vote rescinded.

item.title: Re: Downvoted.
item.content:encoded:
I don't fully understand what you're saying. I actually don't understand why people like it, the intent behind that statement wasn't to be insulting.

I'll Admit I was being a dick with the "all-caps imitation" thing. That was my bad, and I'll hold that L. I was tired and had a rough day at college, but I recognize that that doesn't excuse my actions, it merely explains them. I was being a shit person and all things considered you guys should hold me accountable

I never said people who disagree with me have bad taste. I said that I didn't see what they were seeing. And I understand how someone could see that as a fake rhetorical question, but it actually wasn't.

"What's even the point? "Oh maybe somebody will see this thing and agree with me and downvote it". Why even say something like this for any reason other than be demeaning to the author and everyone who enjoyed the article?"

That wasn't the idea. The idea was (ideally) that someone would see the SCP, make a decision for themselves whether to upvote or downvote, and if they decided to upvote, explain their reasons for disagreeing with me and what they found interesting about it. I now realize that I probably could've worded that better (hence my reply to shaggydredlocks' reply) but that was my intent.

Final point, though; Intent behind ones actions isn't what matters in regards what you put out into the world, it's how the other people interpret your actions. I fucked up. I didn't explain myself properly, I was unnecessarily rude, and overall, I was a dick.

So… fuck well I feel bad saying sorry now so I don't really know how to end this… so, uh, yeah.

item.title: Re: Downvoted.
item.content:encoded:
Which made me feel weird a bit about apologizing for that.

I think what happened was that the apology felt a little disingenuous in your initial post, since so much of said post was material that seemed to make accusations against the article.

Maybe take a quick breather from this discussion for the time being?

item.title: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Not going to call a discussion stop at this moment; however, the above post is unnecessarily vehement. Everyone in this comment chain, please continue discussion civilly and avoid making excessively antagonistic personal comments about others.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright. I was asked to crit this quite some time ago…and I never actually got around to it. At the moment, an overwhelming amount of negative comments here have been largely unhelpful, or focused on inane, superficial 'problems', so I hope I can give something of value.

So, I've read this twice so far, and am about to get into it for a third time as a refresher. After my first read; my first impression was that I loved the cross-links, and figured they were pretty well-done - in my opinion, they were the best part of the SCP. This was troubling, because that's obviously not what I should have gleaned from what's supposed to be a character piece. I honestly had no reaction to most of the story (save mild confusion) - and I was incapable of identifying with your protagonist. Figured out why after my second read:

+ show block
– hide block

So, this doesn't resonate with me for a couple reasons, mostly having to deal with the interviews, and 3090's characterization. I think that a bit too much of the narrative in those logs were consumed with the trite 'unhappiness in containment/wanting freedom' that's been played every which way in plenty of other SCPs. The more original elements - her relationship to her mother and how gaming plays into it - are brushed over for the most part.

This kinda sucks because there could have been more focus on her life/family/upbringing to help bring her to life - as some of her personality and motivation was hard to empathize with. There are a few things about her that seem hypocritical or farcical and I can't really identify with some of her viewpoints:

Said figure held a sign that read "We can be the generation that ends gaming addiction forever".
It's fine for me to play all the time, since I don't need to do anything else, but I made sure she didn't burn herself out.

Though a large part of 3090 is her general dislike for violent games or excessive playing, she's totally kosher with both A) herself playing [which even with the quote above seems hypocritical] and B)Her mom being an avid gamer. I'm not sure where this distaste manifests, so most of her actions to interject on other's playtime/campaign against violence in games/gaming addiction don't seem like they're grounded in reality in any way.

Now, I think that if we get some sound reasoning behind this, it could be stronger. Something more could be said about gaming addiction and how that affected the relationship with her mother…negatively, I'd imagine. She's (her mother) defined at every turn as being surrounded/fixated on gaming: She plays them, streams them, creates them, made 3090 with the intent of making her games have cooler graphics, the only memory 3090 recounts of them leaving the house is for her mother to use her to win a Switch.

A lot of these details could have been elaborated upon to show how 3090 developed this outlook - because as it stands, she doesn't feel authentic to me.

Nitpicks:

+ show block
– hide block

are not allowed in a 12m radius to SCP-3090 except during testing periods.

Should 'to' be 'of'?

replaced then by various text characters assembled into a facsimile of a face, dependent on its mood.

Should it be 'with expressions that change depending on its mood'? Not sure if its supposed to be saying that, or that she may or may not have a facsimile of a face to begin with, dependent on her mood.

This state of separation also renders it unable to communicate or interact with others.

I would consider inserting 'meaningfully', unless she's completely inert, her body should be able to blindly stumble about at the very least - which is some form of interaction.

Playing SCP-3090-1 induces a catatonic state on the player when their avatar or character is terminated or otherwise enters a fail state. This reverts upon resumption of play, or if the instance of SCP-3090-1 ceases operation. Subjects regaining consciousness report feelings of dissociation and identifying with their digital avatar.

I think it would flow better if this were moved beneath the following paragraph that talks about her control over the game's graphics. Right now it hops from dealing with how she affects games, to how players are affected, then back to how she affects games. I would maybe insert an extra line about other phenomena, because the other traits that pop up do come out of left field (mainly the pokemon bit).

graphics in-game to appear slightly more exaggerated.

The WW is already a bit cartoon-y and exaggerated, so I'm not able to form any kind of mental image here - it's a touch too vague.

Aesthetic alterations are variable depending on the subject playing.

No clue what this sentence is doing. It doesn't seem that it's elaborated upon, it just hangs there.

Subjects appeared to have wounds consistent with what would be expected from whatever made their Pokémon faint. SCP-3090 would go choose a new Pokémon for the subject, reversing their state. Text transcribed from D-3015's game follows.

The former sentence is worded rather awkwardly, and I would replace 'go' with 'then'.

I'd try something like:

Wounds began to manifest on subjects which were consistent with the injuries sustained by their respective Pokémon.

Or something better…

SCP-3090: [SCP-3090's head displays an ellipsis, remaining silent for another minute. As Dr. Lichen is about to ask another question it speaks.] It's not exactly an ideal situation, is it? Maybe it's because people are angriest right when they die in a video game, or lose. It cuts down on aggression and hostility so you have the time to cool down and not be aggressive. That's what I assume, anyway. I never bothered to ask.

Lichen: What are your earliest memories? Do you remember who created you?

SCP-3090: My mothers. It feels too weird calling them creators. I stayed in one of their places and got to play all the time. It's fine for me to play all the time, since I don't need to do anything else, but I made sure she didn't burn herself out. Anyway… they wanted to take me to a GameStop and win a video game tournament. Which was fine, since I'm very, very good at gaming. I won, and of course people had all of, uh, that happen to them.

The segue from speaking of the test to speaking of her moms could have been introduced a bit more naturally, I feel a minor switchup would help it flow. Perhaps just switching Lichen's two sentences here would suffice, because it reads like the line of questioning took a random turn.

As an alternative tweak, which is definitely taking some liberties with the exchange:

It cuts down on aggression and hostility so you have the time to cool down and not be aggressive. That's what I assume, anyway. I never bothered to ask.

Lichen: Ask who? You remember who created you?

SCP-3090: My mothers. It feels too weird calling them 'creators'.

Lichen: Very well. Can you tell me about your earliest memories?

Which was fine, since I'm very, very good at gaming. I won, and of course people had all of, uh, that happen to them.

This broke my SoD a bit. I'm not sure how she could be present for a tournament long enough to win without her effects manifesting beforehand and derailing the entire event. Similarly:

One of the other contestants took my head off because they thought it was part of a costume. It didn't come off so I took it off myself. Everyone… freaked out, and when they put it back on me in a panic,

So, people spontaneously losing consciousness didn't set anyone off, but her head removal did?

they locked me out while they called the police. [SCP-3090 sighs.] I tried to follow my mom out but I lost sight of her and ended up locked outside of the store.

I would rephrase the last sentence here somewhat, she kinda repeats herself.

It determined that the victim had [REDACTED], though chemical testing of the substance revealed that it was non-anomalous and able to be replicated. The 3DS also had "contusions from [regular usage]," but this was unrelated to the patient's cause of death.

I would maybe stick quotes around 'victim' and 'patient's' - the way it's framed has the SCPF referring to the game as 890 would.

Lichen: My bad, 3090 — Heather. I thought I had gotten a te —

[SCP-3090's screen immediately changes to a flashing exclamation point. The phone's screen instantly cracks accompanied by the sound of glass breaking.]

Lichen: What —

SCP-3090: Please… please pay attention. This is not the time to play video games.

[Conversation halted for 19 seconds as Lichen handed Yamagusuku his phone, making nervous hums as Yamagusuku leaves the room. Lichen clears his throat before continuing the interview.]

Lichen: Right, sorry. I didn't realize my phone was in my pocket. Please continue.

SCP-3090: [SCP-3090's screen changes to a face with two X's for eyes.] You called me a number. That never sounds nice, regardless of the context. [Silence for twelve seconds, as Yamagusuku returns to the room.] You don't plan to have me leave here, right?

Another section I think could be restructured to flow better, because at first it seems like she's upset at the interruption, but then randomly comes around to the Dr calling her by her designation. I would write it as:

Lichen: Right, sorry. I thought I had gotten a te —

[SCP-3090's screen immediately changes to a flashing exclamation point. The phone's screen instantly cracks accompanied by the sound of glass breaking.]

Lichen: What —

SCP-3090: Please… please pay attention. This is not the time to play video games.

[Conversation halted for 19 seconds as Lichen handed Yamagusuku his phone, making nervous hums as Yamagusuku leaves the room. Lichen clears his throat before continuing the interview.]

Lichen: My bad, 3090 — Heather. I didn't realize my phone was in my pocket. Please continue.

SCP-3090: [SCP-3090's screen changes to a face with two X's for eyes.] You called me a number. That never sounds nice, regardless of the context. [Silence for twelve seconds, as Yamagusuku returns to the room.] You don't plan to have me leave here, right?

item.title: Re: Downvoted.
item.content:encoded:
If you say

sorry if this comes off as mean-spirited

and then say

"LOOK AT HER! SHE'S SO SAD! AND I MADE HER DIE IN A VIDEO GAME REFERENCE I'M SO CLEVER!!!"

then you're not actually sorry.

I don't actually understand the reason for or the value of posts like this. "I can't for the life of me see why people love this thing so much" is such a thinly veiled bold faced insult it's almost laughable, which you've so cleverly hidden under the guise of "I'm not a racist, but".

You're not even asking why people like it, so maybe somebody could clue you in and you'd have a better understanding or appreciation of it. What you're saying is "I think this is garbage, and everybody who disagrees with me has garbage taste." it's fine if you think that, but it displays such an intrinsic lack of self awareness and tact to say those kinds of things out loud.

What's even the point? "Oh maybe somebody will see this thing and agree with me and downvote it". Why even say something like this for any reason other than be demeaning to the author and everyone who enjoyed the article?

Seriously, fuck off with that. You'd be better off blanking these posts and burying your head in the sand and hope nobody ever digs this shit up, because it's a dogshit way to treat other members of this community.

Get good. For fuck's sake.

item.title: Re: Downvoted.
item.content:encoded:
I'll take that, in retrospect I'll admit I was a bit confrontational. I thought about including that in my reply, but they said, quote,
"you could at least not apologize for what you said. You weren't mean-spirited to begin with; saying that only makes your post look more suspicious."
Which made me feel weird a bit about apologizing for that.

item.title: Re: Downvoted.
item.content:encoded:
I don't like the SCP, and from your response you seem to be implying that that's because I'm too stupid to get it? Kind of a dick move.

To be quite fair, using all caps speak in a mocking manner in your intro post came off as pretty vehement, so while that's not the nicest reply in the world, you set yourself off on a bad foot.

item.title: Re: Downvoted.
item.content:encoded:
- I don't have a problem with putting more eyes on this SCP. If people see my post, read the SCP, and like it, good for them! I'm just trying to understand why. I'm not angry at them in any sense of the word.
- I also didn't say that she was a self insert; I said she felt like one. Minor difference, but still.
- I'm sorry if it came off like I was ripping on the thing without reading it… but I did read it. I'll admit, the whole thing is pretty well written and in a couple ways is a good subversion of the "SPOOOOKY VIDEO GAME" trope. But also lives and breathes other tropes, which sometimes make it confusing and a bit all over the place.
" I'll sit here with a (at the time I'm writing this) +78 article, and you can continue to be mystified for why people love it instead of taking even half a minute to be analytical about it."
I don't like the SCP, and from your response you seem to be implying that that's because I'm too stupid to get it? Kind of a dick move. I mean, I'll admit I'm a fucking idiot most of the time, but saying "You just don't like my thing because you don't get it" doesn't seem like a very good point.

item.title: Re: Downvoted.
item.content:encoded:
Sorry if this comes off as mean-spirited

It feels way too much like a self insert

and the stuff that is there just makes it feel like you're pointing at your quirky object head OC

yelling "LOOK AT HER! SHE'S SO SAD! AND I MADE HER DIE IN A VIDEO GAME REFERENCE I'M SO CLEVER!!!"

I just can't for the life of me see why people love this thing so much.

Not that I particularly care for your post, as it brings more attention to my SCP, gets more people to upvote it and like it (this has happened literally every single time this article gets a comment like yours), and brings my works overall more attention, but you could at least not apologize for what you said. You weren't mean-spirited to begin with; saying that only makes your post look more suspicious.

Because you clearly don't want to take the time and effort to read our work with any sort of seriousness and want to just rip on it to begin with, I'm not going to explain to you why I wrote what I wrote, or expect that Ben will explain why he wrote what he wrote. You can keep pretending we wrote """a self-insert""" (if this is a self-insert, it's kind of worrying that we wrote our OC committing suicide, right?) or that it was intentionally designed to be quirky or that it's devoid of any sort of story. I'll sit here with a (at the time I'm writing this) +78 article, and you can continue to be mystified for why people love it instead of taking even half a minute to be analytical about it.

Plus, you've allowed me to write a post which I can link to for every other future comment complaining about this article, so thanks!

item.title: Downvoted.
item.content:encoded:
This SCP is pretty bad in my eyes. It feels way too much like a self insert, has way too many pop culture references (even for a GAW article), and it makes no sense for her to "hate violent video games" and love danganronpa.
It's just… nothing about it is interesting or new, and the stuff that is there just makes it feel like you're pointing at your quirky object head OC and yelling "LOOK AT HER! SHE'S SO SAD! AND I MADE HER DIE IN A VIDEO GAME REFERENCE I'M SO CLEVER!!!"
Sorry if this comes off as mean-spirited, I just can't for the life of me see why people love this thing so much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i love my yandere objecthead uwu

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fran, I don't give two shits what captainsnoop the vaguely popular blogger and his tumblr clique crew think of my shit or this site, but if you're gonna essentially shitpost bad critique onto my article, can you at least be original about it and not copy and paste text posts? Or barring that, attribute your sources? Preferably, do it in a reply to this comment, as these sorts of comments ALWAYS bring me more upvoted from people who enjoy my work. Thanks!

Also, can you do it without resorting to your anti-SJW talking points that you bring out at times? That'd make you read coherent. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really don't understand sometimes. That isn't something that makes me respond "what the fuck?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
creative horror fiction website.

I personally refer to the wiki as an "online sci-fi/horror collaborative writing community" when I talk to other people about it. Much moreso sci-fi than horror for me, given my authored works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You haven't said why you don't think this is a good piece of creative writing. Although if I'm reading the implications of your post correctly, I don't think I need or want to hear it.

You may want to double-check your assumption that this wiki is solely a "horror fiction website". That's not been the case for a long time now (depending on how strictly you define "horror") and this article is not an outlier in terms of tone and subject matter. Indeed, it is well within the thematic and tonal boundaries of most GAW articles / tales. So there's no need to be/act surprised :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I recently read a post on tumblr that said that today's articles are literally “sad lesbian girl with a TV for a head that likes to play animal crossing and hates violent video games that just wants to go home and hug her girlfriend”.

I thought that it was pretty ridiculous satire and not even close to the truth, and told the OP as such.

Turns out that this is an article that literally exists, not an exaggeration in any way, and is also upvoted quite highly, indeed someone wrote this article and submitted it to this creative horror fiction website.

I stand corrected.

What the fuck?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nothing about this gripped or interested me in any way. I didn't care about the effect, I found the interview logs tedious and unedifying, the addition of backstory for more GAW characters did nothing except reinforce how little I know or care about any of the specific personages of that group.

Hopefully it's one of those things I'll come back to in a few months or years and wonder how I had such a thoroughly "enh" reaction to it. Until that time, though, enh. -1

item.title: Admin Post - Closed
item.content:encoded:
Gowby, this is not the first time you've posted an unnecessarily vehement comment seemingly designed to incite negative responses.

Please be more conscientious with your posting from now on. This is an official warning.

Do not reply if you are not a staff member. This is a closed staff post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read your comment and immediately upvoted.

Not sure what the article's about. Maybe I'll read it at some point! xD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dude what could this even be about?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Another decent concept ruined by SJW. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like her. In my head, she sounds like Nikolle Doolin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I got very few of the references, but I enjoyed reading this. +1'd

#notatruegamer

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mobile Task Force Kappa-10 ("Skynet") are responsible for locating entities suspected to be SCP-3090 or in possession of abilities similar to SCP-3090, due to the possibility of SCP-3090's self-termination being a planned containment breach.

They are. It was in the containment procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not comfortable with the article ending with the Foundation possibly not doing anything about the fact that one of their skips just disappeared. Shouldny there be like a note about the Foundation being on the lookout?
ayy

item.title: Translations!
item.content:encoded:
To promote translations of my work and thank all the authors for their hard work, I am linking to them here in addition to my author page.

  • Japanese by C-Dives
  • Chinese by The_Reaper_Kindred

Thank you all!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Do they think they are the character?

In a sense. They identified with the actions they took in the game, believing they physically caused it. It's not a permanent thing, but it's a feeling they get during the time where their body falls unconscious until shortly after they regain it. So like, if you're playing Call of Duty and shooting some people, and you get shot and killed, and then spawn again, for a bit you'll have felt like you were the one shooting. You know you controlled a character who shot some people, but until the feeling wears off (which I'd assume would only be a couple minutes or so), you feel like you shot them, and then you died, and now you're floating in the air but can see your body lying on the ground in front of a television/computer, a game running, until you snap back to reality and, oh, you're conscious again, it's just a game. And it takes a bit for that feeling to wear off.

Like SCP-3090 said, it was probably done with the intention of letting gamers not freak out and get mad, but the anomaly wasn't done quite perfectly. It also has the side effect of this feeling of identifying with the character being different and not so well-pronounced in everyone who tests it. What I imagine is that the Foundation interviews every D-Class to see what their experience is like, and there's no real firm consistency beyond this identification at best. So that's all they really put in the description.

That's not really the problem. The problem is the "heavy metal poisoning" bit. There may be some logical connection, but I'm not getting it and thus this seems like complete nonsense.

http://www.everydayhealth.com/heavy-metal-poisoning/guide/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

they identified with who they played in the game.

This is really the part that confuses me, because I'm not sure what "identifying with the character" means. Do they think they are the character?

Have you read 890? 890 talks about technology like they're humans.

That's not really the problem. The problem is the "heavy metal poisoning" bit. There may be some logical connection, but I'm not getting it and thus this seems like complete nonsense.

Also, that entire test displays 3090's properties differently due to the Foundation fucking with it by introducing a killagent into the mix to see how it works.

Fuck, that's actually really clever.

Everything else I can understand your reasoning for, and that does improve my opinion of the article a bit. However, It still isn't enough to earn my upvote. Maybe after further consideration my opinion will change, who knows.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
oops this needed a collapsible

+ show block
– hide block

No clue what this means, could use some clarification.

I don't understand? Subjects lose consciousness once they reach the fail state in their game, like losing, dying, etc.. When they come to, they have reported to the Foundation dissociation and that they identified with who they played in the game.

general comments about the graphics improvements

Fair, but I also didn't know how to write that into all the test logs without coming across as redundant or bloating the thing. The answer is nearly always "the graphics are Slightly Better according to 3090's whims", except in the 951 crosstest log, where 3090 changes 951 to Lucas from Super Smash Bros.

What? This wasn't part of 3090's effects.

Yes. This is a thing SCP authors often do, where they don't put every single anomalous detail in the description so more can unfold over the rest of the article.

No idea what this person is even trying to say.

Seems self-explanatory to me. Lichen is trying to get an answer for why 3090 causes people to lose consciousness/dissociate.

Possibly missing a word between "3DS" and "observed", also what the fuck

Yep. Fixed.

While I do like how the crosstests were handled here, this is about the point where my suspension of disbelief was broken, and thus I couldn't get invested in the rest of the article.

Have you read 890? 890 talks about technology like they're humans.

Pretty sure the screen cracking and the glass breaking are the same thing, though correct me if I'm wrong on that.

Meant to be "and the sound of glass breaking".

This is just confusing. Revival was caused by death instead of respawning? Is it like an on/off switch? Then why didn't respawning kill the D-Class? The entire effect here is just too confusing.

The death caused Mario to respawn, causing the D-Class to come back. Also, that entire test displays 3090's properties differently due to the Foundation fucking with it by introducing a killagent into the mix to see how it works.

I may or may not be the kind of person who has everything spelled out for them so take this with a grain of salt, but this note is too vague for me to glean any information from.

The note and GameStop card are supposed to be carried with 3090 wherever it spawns (IE, the GameStop); it doesn't need to be explanatory for it. That said, if 3090 "dies", it'll respawn elsewhere to prevent it from being lost just in case something happened to it. 3090's creators didn't really explain this to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So despite the positive reception this has received from others, there are certain things within this article that lead me to novote until I can actually come to some kind of decision about this article.

Quibbles of varying importance
Yes, it's a word

Subjects regaining consciousness report feelings of dissociation and identifying with their digital avatar.

No clue what this means, could use some clarification.

This is usually done in the form minor graphical adjustments not present in the program's code.

Some examples of this would be nice, or at the very least a broad description of what these graphical changes entail.

and changed the graphics in-game to appear slightly more exaggerated.

Exaggerated how

Subjects appeared to have wounds consistent with what would be expected from whatever made their Pokémon faint.

What? This wasn't part of 3090's effects.

Lichen: But what people experience when they lose or die in a game is, well…

No idea what this person is even trying to say.

The 3DS observed to have a pink substance leaking out of it

Possibly missing a word between "3DS" and "observed", also what the fuck

The substance became thicker and more opaque.

whaaat the fuuuuck

According to SCP-890, the 3DS was complaining of symptoms consistent with heavy metal poisoning.

While I do like how the crosstests were handled here, this is about the point where my suspension of disbelief was broken, and thus I couldn't get invested in the rest of the article.

The phone's screen instantly cracks accompanied by the glass breaking.

Pretty sure the screen cracking and the glass breaking are the same thing, though correct me if I'm wrong on that.

When Mario dies, despite death not being caused by SCP-3090's anomaly, D-8052 revived.

This is just confusing. Revival was caused by death instead of respawning? Is it like an on/off switch? Then why didn't respawning kill the D-Class? The entire effect here is just too confusing.

if die, then revive somewhere else

failsafe. sorry we didnt tell you em. hope this helps. heres something else for the trouble if shit ends up happening to you as well; its legit

I may or may not be the kind of person who has everything spelled out for them so take this with a grain of salt, but this note is too vague for me to glean any information from.


TL;DR: The anomalous effects aren't clearly described and events don't flow logically from one to the next.
Current rating: Neutral leaning towards downvote
